jaundice.heroSubtitle: Neonatal jaundice affects 60% of term infants and 80% of preterm infants. This comprehensive guide provides evidence-based information for parents and caregivers.
jaundice.estimatorText: Using the latest live births data from Statistics Canada to estimate jaundice cases. Based on 60% prevalence for term babies and 80% for preterm babies (assuming 15% preterm).
jaundice.whatIsJaundiceText: Bilirubin is produced from the breakdown of hemoglobin when red blood cells reach the end of their lifespan. Newborns produce approximately twice as much bilirubin as adults due to increased red blood cell turnover, immature liver function, and increased enterohepatic circulation. This natural process leads to the visible yellowing of skin and eyes known as jaundice. While most cases resolve naturally, understanding bilirubin metabolism helps parents recognize when medical attention is needed.
jaundice.yellowSkinEyesDesc: The most common sign, usually starting on the face and moving down the body.
jaundice.drowsinessDesc: Your baby may be unusually sleepy or difficult to wake for feedings.
jaundice.poorFeedingDesc: Difficulty with breastfeeding or bottle-feeding is a key sign to monitor.
jaundice.physiologicalJaundiceText: The normal process of RBC breakdown combined with an immature hepatic system. Appears day 2-3 of life, peaks day 5-7, resolves by day 10-14 in term infants.
jaundice.pathologicalJaundiceText: Results from underlying disease or exceeds age-specific treatment thresholds. Red flags include jaundice within first 24 hours, rapidly rising bilirubin (>8.5 μmol/L/hour), or persistence beyond expected timeframe.
jaundice.riskFactorsText: Prematurity, blood group incompatibility, bruising/cephalohematoma, family history of hemolytic disease, exclusive breastfeeding, and gestational age under 38 weeks.
jaundice.clinicalAssessmentText: Visual inspection in bright light, blanching test, and examination of key areas (sclera, gums, blanched skin).
jaundice.measurementMethodsText: Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) provides rapid screening, while serum bilirubin (SBR) remains the gold standard for quantification. TcB is excellent for screening when >50 μmol/L below phototherapy threshold.
jaundice.monitoringGuidelinesText: All babies <35 weeks gestation require SBR measurement. Term infants with visible jaundice need measurement before discharge, with risk-based follow-up at 24-72 hours.
jaundice.phototherapyText: Uses visible light (430-490 nm) to convert unconjugated bilirubin into water-soluble isomers. Standard phototherapy reduces bilirubin by 17-34 μmol/L within 4-6 hours. Intensive phototherapy (doubled intensity) reduces levels by 20-40% within 4-6 hours.
jaundice.exchangeTransfusionText: Reserved for severe hyperbilirubinemia above exchange thresholds or rapidly rising bilirubin despite intensive phototherapy. Removes ~80 mL/kg of infant's blood, replacing with donor RBCs and plasma.
jaundice.feedingSupportText: Adequate feeding is fundamental - promotes bilirubin excretion via stool and prevents dehydration. Lactation consultation recommended for breastfeeding difficulties. Continue breastfeeding during phototherapy.
